questions.question: The `chown --no-dereference` option always acts on the file pointed to by a symbolic link.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. chown --no-dereference option acts on symbolic links themselves instead of what they point to.
questions.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: GNU `chgrp` forbids using a leading `+` when a numeric group ID is intended.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. If group is intended to represent a numeric group ID, chgrp allows it to be specified with a leading '+'.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: With recursive `chmod`, `-H` can traverse a command-line argument that is a symlink to a directory.
questions.explanation: Correct. With recursive chmod, the -H option traverses a command line argument that is a symbolic link to a directory, and this is the default if none of -H, -L, or -P is specified.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: If `chown` is given `owner:` with no group name, the file's group is changed to that owner's login group.
questions.explanation: Correct. If a colon but no group name follows owner, chown changes the group of the files to the owner's login group.
questions.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: When `chown` is not operating recursively, acting on what a symbolic link points to is the default behavior.
questions.explanation: Correct. chown --dereference option acts on what symbolic links point to, and this is the default when not operating recursively.
questions.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: On most systems, `chmod` directly changes the permissions stored on a symbolic link itself.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. chmod command does not change the permissions of symbolic links on most systems.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: During recursive directory traversals, `chmod` always changes the permissions of every symbolic link it encounters.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. chmod command ignores symbolic links encountered during recursive directory traversals.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: The `chown --from` option changes ownership only when the file currently has the specified old-owner attributes.
questions.explanation: Correct. chown --from option changes a file's ownership only if it has the current attributes specified by old-owner.
questions.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: With recursive `chmod`, `-L` refuses to traverse symlinks to directories that are encountered.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. With recursive chmod, the -L option traverses every symbolic link to a directory that is encountered.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: The `chmod` command changes the access permissions of named files.
questions.explanation: Correct. chmod command modifies the access permissions of the named files.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: The `chown --recursive` option recursively changes ownership of directories and their contents.
questions.explanation: Correct. chown --recursive option recursively changes ownership of directories and their contents.
questions.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: The `chown` command can change user ownership, group ownership, or both.
questions.explanation: Correct. chown command modifies the user and/or group ownership of each given file.
questions.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: The `chmod --recursive` option changes only the top directory and never touches its contents.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. chmod --recursive option recursively changes permissions of directories and their contents.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: Any process can change the mode bits of any file as long as the path exists.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. Only a process whose effective user ID matches the user ID of the file, or a process with appropriate privileges, may change the file mode bits.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: The `chmod --reference` option copies the mode from a reference file.
questions.explanation: Correct. chmod --reference option changes each file's mode to be the same as that of the reference file.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: If `chown` is given `:group` with no owner, it changes only the group and acts like `chgrp`.
questions.explanation: Correct. If chown is given :group with the owner omitted, only the group of the files is changed, and chown performs the same function as chgrp.
questions.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: File mode bits can be represented only in symbolic form and not as octal numbers.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. File mode bits can be represented either in symbolic form or as an octal number.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: For command-line symlink arguments, `chmod --dereference` matches the default behavior.
questions.explanation: Correct. chmod --dereference option acts on what symbolic links point to, and this is the default for command line arguments.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: If the reference file is a symbolic link, `chown --reference` uses the owner and group of the symlink itself.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. chown --reference option changes the user and group of each file to match those of the reference file, using the referent if the reference is a symbolic link.
questions.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: The `chgrp --recursive` option recursively changes the group ownership of directories and their contents.
questions.explanation: Correct. chgrp --recursive option recursively changes the group ownership of directories and their contents.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: The `chgrp` command can change a file's user ownership directly when you pass a user name.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. chgrp command modifies the group ownership of each given file or uses the group of an existing reference file.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: When a symbolic link is listed on the command line, `chmod` changes the permissions of the file it points to.
questions.explanation: Correct. For each symbolic link listed on the command line, chmod changes the permissions of the pointed-to file.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: With recursive `chgrp`, `-P` means every symlink to a directory will be traversed, and this is the default traversal mode.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. With recursive chgrp, the -P option does not traverse any symbolic links, and this is the default if none of -H, -L, or -P is specified.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: When `chgrp` is not operating recursively, dereferencing symbolic links is the default behavior.
questions.explanation: Correct. chgrp --dereference option acts on what symbolic links point to, and this is the default when not operating recursively.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: With recursive `chmod`, the `-P` option means symbolic links are not traversed.
questions.explanation: Correct. With recursive chmod, the -P option does not traverse any symbolic links.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: If `chown` is given `owner:group`, it changes only the owner and leaves the group unchanged.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. If chown is given owner:group, the group ownership of the files is changed as well.
questions.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: File mode bits control the kinds of access that users have to a file.
questions.explanation: Correct. File mode bits control the kinds of access that users have to a file.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: The `chmod --preserve-root` option has no effect when a recursive `chmod` operation targets `/`.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. chmod --preserve-root option fails upon attempts to recursively change the root directory '/'.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: If the reference file is a symbolic link, `chgrp --reference` uses the group of the symlink itself rather than the file it refers to.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. chgrp --reference option changes each file's group to match the reference file, using the referent if the reference is a symbolic link.
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.

questions.question: If `chown` is given only `:` or an empty new-owner, it resets both owner and group to defaults.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. If only a colon is given, or if new-owner is empty, neither the owner nor the group is changed.
questions.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
questions.reason: Approved in final review after source and source_ir_id confirmation.
